sources.notes: Statement and constraints: compute the number of ones in an arbitrary range [l, r] of the infinite prefix-XOR-defined binary sequence.
Official editorial generalizes D1's halving recurrence to a recursive prefix-sum function returning even-position and odd-position sums.

reference_candidates.notes: Luogu candidate source; not used in this review because the official editorial was sufficient.

annotation.summary: Given the first n bits of an infinite sequence where later terms are prefix XORs, answer the sum of bits on a huge index range [l, r].
annotation.constraints: n totals 2e5, while r can be 1e18, so the solution needs O(n) preprocessing and logarithmic recursive range queries.
annotation.core_idea: Use the D1 identity for individual terms, but compute prefix sums rather than single values. After normalizing n to odd and precomputing up to 2n, define a recursive sum(m) returning counts on even and odd indices up to m. For m beyond the base region, adjust m to a convenient residue, express the tail in pairs whose two positions contribute equally, and recurse on floor(m/2) to get the needed lower-level even contribution. The answer is sum(r) - sum(l-1).
annotation.complexity: O(n + log r) per test case, O(n) memory.
tricks: Reuse the single-value halving identity from the easy version.
Turn a range query into two recursive prefix-sum queries.
Separate even and odd positions so paired tail contributions can be counted uniformly.

solution_variants.name: recursive-even-odd-prefix-sum
solution_variants.summary: Precompute sequence values and even/odd prefix sums up to 2n. Implement sum(m) = {even ones, odd ones} recursively for huge m by reducing to floor(m/2), then subtract prefix sums to answer [l, r].
solution_variants.complexity: O(n + log r) time, O(n) memory.

tags.evidence: The prefix-sum function for huge m recurses on floor(m/2), just as D1 reduces huge indices by halving.
The range answer is computed as prefix_sum(r) minus prefix_sum(l-1).
The hard version depends on the derived identities for equal pairs and for expressing tail sums through the halved prefix.
The recursive prefix sum separates even and odd positions and adjusts m by residue before applying the pair formula.
The sequence recurrence and all identities use XOR over binary values.
